title: Sudan Archives: How to Build a World With Violin and Chaos
author: Allastair Voss
description: There's a moment in "NBPQ (Topless)" where the violin cuts through everything — production, tempo, voice — and you realize you're listening to something
image_alt: Sudan Archives: How to Build a World With Violin and Chaos
body:
## Sudan Archives: How to Build a World With Violin and Chaos

[img:/assets/images/artists/sudan-archives-natural-brown-prom-queen.jpg]

There is a moment in "NBPQ (Topless)" where the violin cuts through everything, production, tempo, voice, and you realize you are listening to something assembled differently from anything else in the R&B orbit. That is what Sudan Archives does. Cincinnati-born, LA-based Brittney Parks writes music that refuses the usual containers.

Natural Brown Prom Queen, released on Stones Throw Records in 2022, is her most fully realized statement. Across 18 tracks she weaves self-taught violin into trap percussion, traditional West African rhythmic patterns, and electronic textures in a way that sounds both technically complex and physically immediate. "Selfish Soul" hits like a pop song and sounds like an experiment. "Freakalizer" bounces with a kind of joy that most producers would not think to pair with strings. "Home Maker" sits in a slower, more tender register, the violin carrying something the words do not need to say.

The album is named for an experience: the moment when a Black woman walks into a room as she is, without apology. Parks has said she wanted the record to feel like a coronation, not a plea. It does.

## The Violin as Architecture

What is easy to miss on first listen is how technically sophisticated the arrangements are. The violin is not decoration. It is the architecture. Parks builds from it outward, adding layers of bass, synth, and drum machine on top. She sounds less like someone who plays violin in an R&B context and more like someone who rewired R&B through violin to begin with.

Parks taught herself to play partly by listening to music from Sudan and other parts of East Africa, which is where the project's name originates, and that source material is audible in how she approaches the instrument. West African fiddle traditions treat the instrument as a rhythmic voice as much as a melodic one. The bow hits strings in patterns that are percussive and pitched simultaneously. Parks absorbed that approach and pushed it further, running the violin through effects, layering loops, treating it as something that could carry a beat as readily as a sentiment.

The result is arrangements that do not separate their emotional and rhythmic functions in the way that most contemporary R&B does. The strings are not there to swell under a chorus and recede during a verse. They are load-bearing throughout. Take them out and the structure collapses. The bass and drums are built around them, not the other way.

## The Production Logic

Stones Throw Records, known for its Madlib and J Dilla affiliations, its commitment to left-field hip-hop and experimental soul, is the right home for this record in a way that goes beyond marketing logic. The label's aesthetic sensibility has always favored producers who work from the inside of the music outward, who start with a specific sound obsession and build a whole world from it, rather than producers who start with a format and fill it.

Parks fits that lineage exactly. Her production does not start with genre and add personality. It starts with a specific combination of sounds, principally the violin and electronic drum programming, and derives the genre from what those sounds produce when put together. Sometimes that produces something close to R&B. Sometimes it produces something closer to experimental electronic music. On "Freakalizer" it produces something that functions as pure pop without being reducible to it.

The 18-track scope is a deliberate statement about range. Not every track hits equally hard. That is not a flaw. The album needs space to breathe and shift register, and the tracks that step back from intensity make the ones that charge forward land harder. The sequencing is intelligent in this regard. Parks knows when to press and when to let a moment settle.

## The Coronation

The album title refers to a specific kind of self-possession. The prom queen is a figure from American cultural life who is anointed by external consensus, whose value is determined by whether others vote for her. Parks is naming a different kind of coronation, one that does not require external validation, one that is simply the act of showing up fully as yourself and not asking permission.

That framing is most audible on "NBPQ (Topless)" and "Selfish Soul," the tracks where Parks's voice is most central and most unguarded. The lyrics are not making arguments about representation or rehearsing the terms of Black feminist discourse. They are describing specific experiences, specific feelings, specific moments of self-recognition. The political content, and it is political, emerges from that specificity rather than from declaration.

This is the harder and more effective approach. A song that says "I am enough" is a slogan. A song that describes exactly what it feels like to be exactly who you are, with all the violin and trap drums and West African fiddle influence that entails, is evidence. Parks provides evidence.

## Three Years On

Natural Brown Prom Queen still sounds ahead of the conversation. The R&B landscape has continued to absorb influences from African music, from electronic production, from experimental approaches to strings and voice. None of it has caught up to what Parks did on this record in 2022.

That is partly because the record is so specific to her particular combination of influences and skills that it resists imitation. You cannot reproduce the sound without reproducing the person who made it, the specific training, the specific obsessions, the specific way of hearing the violin as a rhythmic instrument before it is a melodic one.

Parks is working on what comes next, and the anticipation is genuine. She set a standard with this record that her own subsequent work will have to reckon with. That is a good problem to have.
